chemicals.chemical_formula: Not provided in the given sources.
chemicals.concentration_notes: Pothos toxicity is generally classified as moderate1. The insoluble oxalate crystals are scattered throughout the entire plant45. However, because the crystals cause such immediate and intense pain the second the cat bites into the plant, the animal is usually deterred from eating large quantities24. As a result, Pothos poisonings are rarely fatal, and full recovery is typically expected
chemicals.description: in cats: The mechanism of action is primarily physical trauma. These microscopic, needle-like crystals are bundled together inside specialized plant cells known as idioblasts3. When a cat bites, chews, or crushes the leaves or stems of the Pothos plant, these cells forcefully eject the sharp crystals directly into the cat's oral mucosa and surrounding tissues23. This microscopic piercing causes im
chemicals.name: Insoluble calcium oxalate crystals (often referred to as raphides)

description: Pothos (Epipremnum aureum), also called Devil’s Ivy, is one of the most common houseplants and a frequent cause of plant-related toxicity in cats. As a member of the Araceae family, it contains insoluble calcium oxalate crystals (raphides) throughout its leaves and stems. Chewing the plant causes immediate intense oral pain and irritation.
name: Pothos / Devil’s Ivy
safetyNotes: Toxic compounds: insoluble calcium oxalate raphides; sap also contains proteolytic enzymes that intensify the reaction
Mechanism: crystals are forcefully ejected into the oral mucosa when plant cells rupture, causing mechanical burning and swelling
Symptoms: sudden oral pain, intense drooling, pawing at mouth, difficulty swallowing, swollen lips and tongue, vomiting
The trailing vines are very accessible to cats, especially when draped over furniture or shelves
Rinse the mouth with water; seek veterinary care if swallowing is impaired or symptoms do not subside

symptoms.name: Intense burning, irritation, and pain of the mouth, lips, and tongue
symptoms.notes: This immediate and intense pain occurs because Pothos contains microscopic, needle-like insoluble calcium oxalate crystals (raphides)12. When a cat bites or chews the leaves or stems, these crystals are forcefully ejected directly into the oral mucosa2. Because the pain is instantaneous, cats are us

symptoms.name: Excessive drooling or hypersalivation
symptoms.notes: This is a direct physical and inflammatory response to the microscopic trauma and irritation caused by the oxalate crystals piercing the cat's mouth12. 3. Oral Swelling and Difficulty Swallowing

symptoms.name: Swelling of the mouth and tongue, accompanied by difficulty swallowing (dysphagia)
symptoms.notes: The localized inflammatory response can cause the cat's mouth and tongue to become significantly swollen23. In severe cases, this swelling can progress to the throat and potentially obstruct the cat's airway, which requires immediate veterinary attention12. 4. Vomiting

symptoms.name: Vomiting
symptoms.notes: While the systemic toxicity of Pothos is relatively low and not typically life-threatening, the ingestion of the plant material and the resulting irritation cause extreme gastrointestinal discomfort and stomach upset

treatments.description: If the severe inflammatory response causes laryngeal edema (swelling of the throat and airway), the veterinarian will immediately intervene to secure the cat's airway and provide oxygen support
treatments.name: Airway Management and Oxygen Supplementation
treatments.notes: This is the most critical, life-threatening emergency associated with Pothos ingestion. If you notice your cat having any difficulty breathing or significant swelling of the mouth or throat, they must be taken to a veterinary emergency clinic immediately12. 2. Oral Decontamination

treatments.description: Gently removing any remaining plant pieces from the cat's mouth and thoroughly rinsing or wiping out the oral cavity with cool water to flush away the sharp, embedded calcium oxalate crystals
treatments.name: Oral Decontamination (Removal and Rinsing)
treatments.notes: This is an immediate first-aid step to stop the ongoing microscopic trauma to the cat's tissues. You should never induce vomiting for Pothos ingestion unless specifically instructed by a vet, as bringing the sharp crystals back up can cause further severe pain and damage to the esophagus3. 3. Calciu

treatments.description: Offering the cat a small amount of a calcium-containing dairy product, such as milk or yogurt
treatments.name: Oral Calcium Administration
treatments.notes: The insoluble oxalate crystals will actively bind to the calcium in the dairy product. This helps comfortably clear the remaining crystals from the oral cavity and esophagus, significantly reducing the intense burning sensation1. 4. Symptomatic and Supportive Care

treatments.description: The veterinarian may administer medications such as analgesics (pain relievers) to soothe the severe mouth pain, or demulcents to coat and protect the irritated mucous membranes4. Anti-nausea medications may also be given if the cat is experiencing secondary vomiting.
treatments.name: Symptomatic Pain and Gastrointestinal Management
treatments.notes: Because the intense pain usually deters the cat from eating a large amount of the plant, full systemic poisoning is highly unlikely.
